items: Rational numbers: can be written as p/q, q ≠ 0

data.title: Memory helper
data.body: N ⊂ W ⊂ Z ⊂ Q ⊂ R. Irrational numbers sit inside R but outside Q.

data.caption: Use this image as a quick revision card for set nesting.

clarifiers.title: Exam tip
clarifiers.body: If you freeze in an exam, write the subset chain first. Then place the number in the smallest suitable set.

questions.prompt: In a Venn diagram representing number systems, which set forms the innermost, smallest circle?
questions.explanation: The natural numbers (counting numbers) form the most basic, restrictive set, which is contained within all other larger sets.
questions.supportHint: Which set has the fewest types of numbers?

questions.prompt: Why is it impossible for a number to be both rational and irrational at the same time?
questions.explanation: A number either CAN be written as p/q (rational) or CANNOT be written as p/q (irrational). It cannot be both.
questions.supportHint: Look at the definition of irrational.

questions.prompt: Classify the number of days in a standard non-leap year (365) into as many number sets as possible.
questions.explanation: 365 is a positive counting number, which automatically makes it part of all the larger encompassing sets in the hierarchy.
questions.supportHint: Start from the smallest set it belongs to.

questions.prompt: Assertion: All terminating decimals are real numbers. Reason: All terminating decimals are rational numbers, and all rational numbers are real numbers.
questions.answer: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation.
questions.explanation: This traces the subset relationship perfectly.

questions.prompt: Which set is exactly equivalent to the union of negative integers and whole numbers?
questions.explanation: Whole numbers contain 0 and positives. Adding negatives completes the set of integers.
questions.supportHint: What do you get when you combine positives, zero, and negatives?

questions.prompt: The intersection of the set of Rational numbers and the set of Irrational numbers is the empty set.
questions.explanation: A number cannot be both rational (can be a fraction) and irrational (cannot be a fraction) simultaneously.
questions.supportHint: Can you be in two opposite places at once?

questions.prompt: A number is classified as a Real number and an Integer, but NOT a Natural number. What are the possible values for this number?
questions.explanation: The only integers that are not natural numbers are 0, -1, -2, -3...
questions.supportHint: What is excluded from the natural set?

questions.prompt: If a number is placed in the 'Rational' section of a Venn diagram, does it have to be inside the 'Integer' section too?
questions.explanation: Numbers like 1/2 are rational but sit outside the integer circle.
